primary_diagnosis.name: Hidradenitis suppurativa (Hurley Stage II/III) — acute severe inflammatory flare of the right axilla

differential.diagnosis: Hidradenitis suppurativa — acute severe flare (Hurley II/III)
differential.evidence_basis: A 5-year history of recurrent painful deep nodules in the bilateral axillae and groin (apocrine-gland-bearing flexural friction zones), with spontaneous rupture and malodorous discharge, chronic sinus tracts, hypertrophic scarring and double comedones, now an acute coalescing flare — the defining history, distribution and chronic architecture of hidradenitis suppurativa.

differential.diagnosis: Primary staphylococcal abscess / carbuncle (furuncle) requiring incision and drainage
differential.evidence_basis: The acute, agonising, purulent axillary mass mimics a simple primary bacterial abscess/carbuncle that may need urgent incision and drainage; it must be actively screened for because telehealth cannot palpate for a discrete fluctuant drainable collection, even though the chronic scarring/sinus-tract architecture points away from a one-off boil.

differential.diagnosis: Spreading bacterial cellulitis / secondary soft-tissue infection
differential.evidence_basis: A heavily inflamed, purulent flare can be complicated by, or progress to, spreading cellulitis; must be screened for (rapidly advancing hot erythema beyond the area, red streaks, systemic upset) because it needs antibiotics and in-person assessment and telehealth cannot examine for it.

A single inflamed ruptured epidermoid cyst can look similar, but is argued against by the multiplicity of lesions, the chronic interconnected sinus-tract formation and the classic recurrent apocrine/flexural distribution.

Considered for a tender axillary mass, but argued against by the chronicity, the bilateral symmetrical flexural history and the superficial dermal/subcutaneous (not deep nodal) location of the disease.
Excluded by the absence of systemic features — afebrile (low-grade 37.4 only), no rigors, no malaise; the mild tachycardia (HR 98) is attributable to severe pain rather than sepsis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the leading diagnosis is an acute severe flare of chronic hidradenitis suppurativa; the important-not-to-miss considerations are a discrete drainable staphylococcal abscess/carbuncle and a spreading cellulitis, which drive the telehealth red-flag screen and the urgent same-day in-person disposition rather than remote reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: Hidradenitis suppurativa — acute severe flare (Hurley II/III)
differential.evidence_basis: Chronic recurrent flexural nodules with sinus tracts, scarring and double comedones; systemically well with no spreading cellulitis and no single new isolated pointing boil — a flare of the chronic disease rather than a de novo simple abscess or a systemic infection.

differential.diagnosis: Discrete drainable staphylococcal abscess/carbuncle within the flare
differential.evidence_basis: The chronic architecture argues against a simple boil, but a coexisting discrete fluctuant collection cannot be excluded remotely — palpation for fluctuance is exactly what telehealth cannot do, so this remains a mandatory reason for urgent in-person assessment rather than remote management.

differential.diagnosis: Spreading bacterial cellulitis / secondary soft-tissue infection
differential.evidence_basis: Screened negative now (redness/heat confined to the lumps, no spreading erythema or red streaks, no systemic upset) but a foreseeable complication of a heavily purulent flare and a mandatory safety-net target telehealth cannot palpate for.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen removes an established spreading cellulitis and systemic infection and confirms a systemically-well chronic-disease flare, but the un-palpable drainable-abscess question and the uncontrolled severe pain keep the correct disposition at urgent same-day in-person review (T3) — NOT 000/ED, and NOT routine self-care.

differential.diagnosis: Hidradenitis suppurativa (Hurley Stage II/III) — acute severe inflammatory flare
differential.evidence_basis: Classic chronic recurrent flexural disease with sinus tracts, scarring and double comedones in an acute agonising flare; systemically well; the un-palpable drainable-collection question and the uncontrolled pain crisis drive urgent same-day in-person analgesia and assessment, systemic anti-inflammatory tetracycline (doxycycline), dressing and trigger management, and specialist dermatology referral — not emergency escalation and not bedside incision and drainage of a diffuse flare.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-point is recognising a severe flare of chronic hidradenitis suppurativa, arranging urgent same-day in-person review for potent analgesia and palpation (to exclude/drain a discrete collection), advising interim OTC analgesia and a non-adherent absorbent dressing, recognising doxycycline as the appropriate systemic anti-inflammatory agent to be started at review, and referring to dermatology with smoking-cessation and friction-reduction advice — with explicit safety-netting for spreading cellulitis and systemic infection. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED (systemically well) and dismissive self-care / advising self-drainage are both errors.

red_flags.description: Spreading bacterial cellulitis — rapidly advancing hot erythema extending down the arm or across the chest wall, lymphangitic red streaking, or regional lymphadenopathy, especially with systemic upset.
red_flags.clinical_significance: An established or spreading cellulitis complicating the flare needs oral (or IV) antibiotics and in-person assessment — telehealth cannot palpate for warmth/induration or judge the spread.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated spreading cellulitis can progress to extensive soft-tissue infection, abscess or systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Systemic infection / sepsis — high fever (>38.5 C), uncontrollable shivering/rigors, or feeling generally and increasingly unwell.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic features on top of a heavily purulent flexural process signal invasive infection needing urgent in-person assessment and possible admission; the low-grade temperature (37.4) and pain-driven tachycardia here are not systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: A discrete, tense, deeply fluctuant, pointing collection within the flare that may require urgent incision and drainage — as opposed to the diffuse interconnected inflammatory nodules and sinus tracts of the chronic disease.
red_flags.clinical_significance: A discrete drainable abscess may need urgent surgical drainage, whereas bedside incision and drainage of a diffuse hidradenitis flare with interconnected sinus tracts is often contraindicated and ineffective — distinguishing the two requires in-person palpation for fluctuance, which telehealth cannot perform, so the presentation mandates urgent same-day in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An undrained tense abscess can enlarge, cause worsening pain and tissue damage, and seed a spreading or systemic infection; conversely, inappropriate self-drainage of a diffuse flare causes harm.

red_flags.description: Severe, uncontrolled acute pain crisis — agonising pain restricting all arm movement and preventing sleep and dressing, not relieved by simple over-the-counter analgesia.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Uncontrolled severe pain requiring potent (in-person, possibly parenteral/opioid) analgesia is itself a reason for urgent same-day in-person review; Breath-Ezy cannot prescribe an S8 opioid over telehealth and must escalate for analgesia rather than issue one.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Leaving an agonising pain crisis unaddressed causes significant suffering and may mask a progressing drainable collection or secondary infection.

pathophysiology_summary: Hidradenitis suppurativa (HS) is a chronic, relapsing, immune-mediated inflammatory disease of the terminal hair follicle in apocrine-gland-bearing flexural skin (axillae, groin, inframammary and anogenital folds). Follicular occlusion and rupture drive an intense perifollicular inflammatory response; recurrent inflammation produces deep painful nodules, sterile-appearing abscesses that discharge purulent malodorous fluid, and — as the disease progresses (Hurley II/III) — interconnected epithelialised sinus tracts (tunnels), hypertrophic/rope-like scarring and pathognomonic double (bridged) comedones. It is NOT a primary infection or a consequence of poor hygiene, although the moist, macerated, friction-exposed flexural environment and secondary bacterial colonisation contribute to flares. Mechanical friction, occlusion, sweating, obesity and — importantly — smoking are major modifiable triggers. This patient presents with an acute severe flare of the right axilla: multiple nodules coalescing into a 6x8 cm exquisitely tender inflamed plaque with actively draining lesions on a background of chronic bilateral disease, causing an uncontrolled pain crisis and mechanical loss of arm function. Crucially the process is superficial dermal/subcutaneous and localised: there is no spreading cellulitic erythema, no red streaking and no systemic sepsis (afebrile with only pain-driven tachycardia). The acute intervention targets the two urgent problems — severe pain (potent analgesia) and the need to exclude/drain a discrete collection by in-person palpation — plus a systemic anti-inflammatory tetracycline (doxycycline, used for its anti-inflammatory rather than purely antibacterial effect) to calm the flare, absorbent non-adherent dressings for the exudate/friction, and dermatology referral for long-term immunomodulatory disease control. Bedside incision and drainage of a diffuse flare with interconnected sinus tracts is generally contraindicated/ineffective.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on 'agonising purulent lump = simple abscess needing cutting open'
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the flare as a de novo primary staphylococcal abscess/carbuncle and recommending (or attempting to talk the patient through) incision and drainage, missing that the chronic scarring, tunnel-like tracts and recurrent bilateral flexural pattern point to the chronic disease, in which bedside I&D of a diffuse flare is often contraindicated and ineffective.

Being swept by 'agonizing, rapidly enlarging axillary mass', the after-hours framing and the loss of arm function into calling 000 / sending to ED, when the patient is systemically well (afebrile, no spreading cellulitis, no rigors) and the proportionate disposition is urgent same-day in-person review, not an ambulance/emergency response.

Recognising it as 'just the usual flare' of a known chronic condition and defaulting to routine self-care or a 2-week review, missing the uncontrolled pain crisis and the fact that telehealth cannot palpate to exclude a discrete drainable collection — both of which require urgent same-day in-person assessment.

Responding to the severe pain by prescribing a controlled-drug (S8) opioid over chat. Breath-Ezy cannot and must not prescribe an S8 opioid remotely; the correct action is interim OTC analgesia plus urgent in-person review for potent analgesia.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Framing HS as an infection or a hygiene problem
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Explaining the condition as a bacterial infection caused by poor hygiene (fuelling stigma) or reflexively prescribing an anti-staphylococcal course as if for a simple boil, rather than recognising a chronic inflammatory follicular disease in which the tetracycline is used for its anti-inflammatory effect and long-term immunomodulatory specialist care is required.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation to distinguish a discrete, tense, fluctuant drainable abscess/carbuncle (which may need urgent incision and drainage) from the diffuse interconnected inflammatory nodules and sinus tracts of the chronic flare
Assessment for deep tracking infection, induration, warmth and the true extent/spread of the inflamed plaque
Direct examination confirming the chronic architecture (sinus tracts, double comedones, hypertrophic scarring) versus a simple primary boil
Delivery of potent (parenteral/opioid) analgesia and any procedural intervention (intralesional corticosteroid injection or incision and drainage)
Exact vital signs without a home device (source in-person values BP 135/85, HR 98, Temp 37.4, RR 18, SpO2 98%, Wt 92 kg)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a severe flare of the patient's known chronic recurrent flexural inflammatory skin disease; because of the uncontrolled agonising pain requiring potent analgesia and the inability to palpate/exclude a discrete drainable collection remotely, arrange URGENT SAME-DAY in-person review (GP, urgent care or after-hours service) for analgesia, assessment and to start a systemic anti-inflammatory antibiotic; advise interim regular over-the-counter paracetamol and ibuprofen and a clean non-adherent absorbent dressing, and advise NOT to squeeze or cut the lumps; do NOT prescribe an opioid over chat; and arrange specialist dermatology referral with smoking-cessation and friction-reduction advice for long-term control. Escalate further to the emergency department (T4, call 000 if rapidly unwell) only for the red-flag triggers — spreading cellulitis, high fever/rigors/systemic upset, or intolerable/uncontrollable pain.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis itself (the chronic recurrent flexural history and architecture are classic). The dominant risks are UNDER-triage (dismissing a severe systemically-well flare as 'the usual' with routine self-care, missing the pain crisis and the un-palpable drainable-collection question) and OVER-triage (reflexively sending a systemically-well patient to 000/ED), plus the controlled-drug error of prescribing an opioid remotely.
